title: Two Sum
question: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.
answer: Optimal uses Hash Map for O(n) lookup.
explanation: Brute force takes O(n^2). Using a Hash Map reduces time to O(n).

title: Valid Anagram
question: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise.
explanation: Sorting takes O(n log n) time. Counting character frequencies in a hash map takes O(n) time and O(1) extra space.

title: Maximum Subarray
question: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum.
explanation: Brute force checks all subarrays O(n^2). Kadane's keeps a running sum and resets if it drops below the current element.

title: Contains Duplicate
question: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return false if every element is distinct.
answer: Using a Set reduces lookup to O(1).
explanation: A Set only stores unique values, allowing us to find duplicates in O(n) time instead of O(n^2).

title: Buy and Sell Stock
question: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day. Maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy and a different day in the future to sell.
answer: Track the minimum price seen so far.
explanation: Instead of checking every pair O(n^2), we just remember the lowest price in the past and check current profit O(n).

title: Valid Parentheses
question: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.
answer: Use a Stack to track open brackets.
explanation: String replacement takes O(n^2) worst-case. A Stack processes each character exactly once for O(n) time.

title: Reverse Linked List
question: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the reversed list.
explanation: Storing values takes O(n) extra space. Reversing pointers in a single pass uses O(1) space.

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
question: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists list1 and list2. Merge the two lists into one sorted list.
explanation: Sorting combined values is O((n+m) log(n+m)). Splicing nodes compares elements in a single pass O(n+m).

title: Binary Search
question: Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search target in nums.
answer: Divide and conquer halves the space.
explanation: Linear search takes O(n). Binary search on a sorted array takes O(log n) time, which is vastly faster for large arrays.

title: Climbing Stairs
question: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?
explanation: Recursive branching takes O(2^n) time! Storing the last two results reduces it to O(n) time and O(1) space.

title: Longest Substring No Repeats
question: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
explanation: Checking every substring is O(n^3). Sliding window maintains a valid substring dynamically in O(n) time.

title: 3Sum
question: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and they add up to 0.
explanation: Brute force is O(n^3). Sorting takes O(n log n) and Two Pointers takes O(n^2), handling duplicates cleanly.

title: Number of Islands
question: Given an m x n 2D binary grid grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands.
bruteForceCode:
// Brute force not practical for graphs.
// Iterating and collecting connected components usually requires DFS/BFS.
explanation: Whenever you encounter a '1', trigger DFS to sink ('0') all connected land masses, then increment island count.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
question: There is an integer array nums sorted in ascending order (with distinct values) that is possibly rotated. Find the index of target.
explanation: Linear search is O(n). By determining which half of the array is properly sorted, we can still achieve O(log n) time.

title: Invert Binary Tree
question: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree, and return its root.
explanation: Visit each node once and swap its left and right children. This takes O(n) time where n is the number of nodes.

title: Linked List Cycle
question: Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it.
explanation: Using a Set requires O(n) space. Floyd's algorithm uses two pointers moving at different speeds, taking O(1) space and O(n) time.

title: Merge k Sorted Lists
question: You are given an array of k linked-lists lists, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it.
explanation: Sorting all values is O(N log N) where N is total nodes. Divide and conquer merges pairs of lists in O(N log k) time.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
question: Given a binary search tree (BST), find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) node of two given nodes in the BST.
explanation: If both p and q are greater than root, LCA must be in the right subtree. If both are less, it's in the left. Otherwise, root is the LCA. O(h) time.

title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
question: A trie or prefix tree is a tree data structure used to efficiently store and retrieve keys in a dataset of strings. Implement the Trie class.
explanation: Array storage takes O(n) for searches. A Trie allows O(L) time complexity for inserts and searches, where L is word length.

title: Word Search
question: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid.
explanation: Start a DFS from every matching first character. Temporarily mark cells as visited ('#') and backtrack if a path fails.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Array
question: Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array.
explanation: Linear scan takes O(n). We can find the pivot in O(log n) by comparing mid to right pointer. If mid > right, min is in the right half.

title: Product of Array Except Self
question: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i].
explanation: Division operator is forbidden. Compute the prefix product in one pass, then multiply by postfix product in a second pass. O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
question: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.
explanation: Sorting the hash map takes O(N log N). Bucket sort places elements into arrays based on their frequency count, yielding O(N) time.

title: Clone Graph
question: Return a deep copy (clone) of a graph. Each node in the graph contains a value and a list of its neighbors.
explanation: Graph can have cycles! Use a Hash Map to map original nodes to cloned nodes to avoid infinite loops. O(V+E) time.

title: Container With Most Water
question: You are given an integer array height of length n. Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water.
explanation: Brute force checks all pairs O(n^2). Start pointers at the ends to maximize width, then move the pointer with the smaller height. O(n) time.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
question: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.
explanation: Checking all substrings takes O(n^3). Expanding around every possible center takes O(n^2) time and O(1) space.

title: Coin Change
question: You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount representing a total amount of money. Return the fewest number of coins that you need to make up that amount.
explanation: Build up the minimum coins needed for every amount from 0 to target. Time complexity is O(amount * coins.length).

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
question: Given an m x n integer matrix heights representing the height of each unit cell in a continent. Return a list of grid coordinates where water can flow to both the Pacific and Atlantic oceans.
bruteForceCode:
// Brute force would DFS from EVERY single cell to see if it reaches both oceans.
// Extremely inefficient, recalculates same paths.
explanation: Instead of starting from land, start from the ocean borders and climb UP. Cells visited by both ocean traversals are the answer. O(m*n).

title: Course Schedule
question: There are a total of numCourses courses you have to take. You are given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [a, b] indicates you must take b first. Return true if you can finish all courses.
bruteForceCode:
// Cycle detection without caching (memoization) leads to O(V!) or infinite loops.
// Graph problems require systematic traversal.
explanation: Convert prerequisites to an Adjacency List. Use DFS to detect cycles. If a cycle exists, you can't finish the courses.

title: Decode Ways
question: A message containing letters from A-Z can be encoded into numbers using 'A' -> '1', 'B' -> '2', ... 'Z' -> '26'. Given a string s containing only digits, return the number of ways to decode it.
explanation: Recursive branching takes O(2^n). Since the current answer only depends on the previous two answers (like Fibonacci), we can solve it in O(n) time and O(1) space.

title: Jump Game
question: You are given an integer array nums. You are initially positioned at the array's first index, and each element in the array represents your maximum jump length at that position. Return true if you can reach the last index.
explanation: Recursion is O(2^n). Instead, work backwards! Shift the goal post closer to the start whenever a jump can reach the current goal. O(n) time.

title: Word Break
question: Given a string s and a dictionary of strings wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.
explanation: Pure recursion repeats checks exponentially. Use DP array to track if a valid segmentation exists starting from index i. O(n * m * L) time.

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
question: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth. The depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
bruteForceCode: // A tree traversal requires either DFS or BFS. There isn't a 'naive' iteration without a stack/queue.
explanation: At every node, the maximum depth is simply 1 (for the current node) plus the maximum of the depth of the left subtree and right subtree.

title: Same Tree
question: Given the roots of two binary trees p and q, write a function to check if they are the same or not.
explanation: Check the current nodes. If they match, recursively verify that the left subtrees match and the right subtrees match. O(n) time.

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
question: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, level by level).
bruteForceCode: // Recursive DFS can simulate this by passing the depth, but Breadth-First Search is the natural approach.
explanation: Use a Queue to process nodes level by level. Keep track of the queue length at the start of each level to group values correctly.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
question: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).
explanation: Pass down the valid range (min, max) to each subtree. If any node violates its inherited bounds, it's not a BST. O(n) time.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
question: Given the root of a binary search tree, and an integer k, return the kth smallest value (1-indexed) of all the values of the nodes in the tree.
explanation: Sorting all nodes takes O(N log N). An in-order traversal of a BST visits nodes in strictly increasing order. Stop when you hit k. O(H + k) time.

title: Construct Tree from Preorder & Inorder
question: Given two integer arrays preorder and inorder where preorder is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and inorder is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.
answer: Divide and Conquer with Hash Map.
explanation: Preorder gives the root. Inorder gives the size of the left and right subtrees. A Hash Map allows O(1) index lookups, yielding O(N) total time.

title: Non-overlapping Intervals
question: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], return the minimum number of intervals you need to remove to make the rest of the intervals non-overlapping.
answer: Greedy approach sorted by End Time.
explanation: Always pick the interval that ends earliest to leave maximum room for future intervals. If an interval overlaps, drop it. O(N log N) time.

title: Meeting Rooms II
question: Given an array of meeting time intervals consisting of start and end times, find the minimum number of conference rooms required.
explanation: Sort starts and ends separately. Iterate through starts: if a meeting starts before the earliest end time, we need a new room. Otherwise, a room freed up. O(N log N).

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
question: Given an m x n integer matrix matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0's. You must do it in place.
answer: Use first row/col as memory flags.
explanation: Copying takes O(m*n) space. Using the first row and column to store which rows/cols should be zeroed brings space complexity down to O(1).

title: Spiral Matrix
question: Given an m x n matrix, return all elements of the matrix in spiral order.
explanation: Maintain 4 boundaries (top, bottom, left, right). Traverse the perimeter, then shrink the boundaries inward. O(m*n) time.

title: Rotate Image
question: You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image, rotate the image by 90 degrees (clockwise) in-place.
explanation: Copying requires O(N^2) space. Flipping across the diagonal (transposing) and then reversing each row achieves the rotation in-place (O(1) space).

title: Min Stack
question: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time.
answer: Store Current Minimum at Each Node.
explanation: Scanning for the minimum takes O(n). By storing what the minimum *was* at the time each element was added, getMin() becomes O(1).

title: House Robber
question: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Adjacent houses have security systems connected and it will contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night. Return the maximum amount of money you can rob.
explanation: Pure recursion is O(2^n). Because you only need the previous two max values, you can compute it in a single pass O(n) time and O(1) space.

title: House Robber II
question: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. All houses at this place are arranged in a circle. Return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.
explanation: Because it's a circle, the first and last house cannot both be robbed. Run the linear House Robber logic twice: once skipping the first house, once skipping the last.

title: Palindromic Substrings
question: Given a string s, return the number of palindromic substrings in it.
explanation: Brute force checks all substrings in O(n^3). Expanding around every character and between every character takes O(n^2) time and O(1) space.

title: Decode String
question: Given an encoded string, return its decoded string. The encoding rule is: k[encoded_string], where the encoded_string inside the square brackets is being repeated exactly k times.
bruteForceCode: // Regular expressions could potentially be used recursively, but it's complex and slow.
explanation: Whenever you hit a closing bracket, pop characters until you find an opening bracket, then pop the number. Multiply and push the result back onto the stack.

title: Subarray Sum Equals K
question: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, return the total number of subarrays whose sum equals to k.
explanation: Brute force is O(n^2). Instead, keep a running prefix sum and track its frequencies in a map. If (currentSum - k) exists in map, add its frequency to count. O(n).

title: Find All Anagrams in a String
question: Given two strings s and p, return an array of all the start indices of p's anagrams in s.
explanation: Sorting takes O(K log K) per window. Maintaining a frequency array inside a sliding window drops the complexity to O(N).

title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
question: You are given a string s and an integer k. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. Perform this operation at most k times to find the longest substring containing the same letter.
bruteForceCode: // Brute force would generate all substrings and test if they can be made uniform with k changes.
explanation: Maintain a frequency map. The condition `windowLength - maxFrequencyInWindow <= k` ensures the window is valid. Expand right, shrink left if invalid. O(n) time.

title: Group Anagrams
question: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.
answer: Hash Map with Character Count Key.
explanation: Sorting each string as a key takes O(K log K). Using an array of character counts as the map key is O(K) per string, drastically improving performance.

title: Merge Intervals
question: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals.
bruteForceCode: // If intervals aren't sorted, you'd have to repeatedly scan the entire array for overlaps, leading to O(n^2).
explanation: Sort by starting times first. Then iterate through: if the current start is less than or equal to the previous end, they overlap—merge them. O(n log n) time.

title: Insert Interval
question: You are given an array of non-overlapping intervals sorted by start time. Insert newInterval into the array, merging if necessary.
explanation: Since it's already sorted, just iterate once. Add non-overlapping intervals, and if they overlap, greedily expand the newInterval boundaries. O(n) time.

title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
question: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.
explanation: Counting the list takes two passes. Use a right pointer shifted n nodes ahead, then move both. When right hits the end, left is at the target. One pass!

title: Reorder List
question: You are given the head of a singly linked-list. The list can be represented as L0 -> L1 -> ... -> Ln-1 -> Ln. Reorder the list to be on the following form: L0 -> Ln -> L1 -> Ln-1 -> L2 -> Ln-2 -> ...
explanation: Storing nodes in an array takes O(N) space. Finding the middle, reversing the second half in-place, and merging the two halves takes O(1) space.

title: Maximum Product Subarray
question: Given an integer array nums, find a subarray that has the largest product, and return the product.
explanation: A negative number multiplied by a negative minimum becomes the new maximum! Keep track of both running maximum and minimum. O(N) time.

title: Search a 2D Matrix
question: You are given an m x n integer matrix matrix with the following two properties: Each row is sorted in non-decreasing order. The first integer of each row is greater than the last integer of the previous row. Given an integer target, return true if target is in matrix.
explanation: Because the rows are strictly sorted sequentially, the 2D matrix can be treated as a single sorted 1D array of length m*n. O(log(m*n)) time.

title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
question: You are given an array of strings tokens that represents an arithmetic expression in a Reverse Polish Notation. Evaluate the expression.
bruteForceCode: // Brute force would require scanning for operators, substituting values, and rewriting the array multiple times O(N^2).
explanation: RPN is designed perfectly for Stacks. Push numbers; when you see an operator, pop two numbers, apply it, and push the result. O(N) time.

title: Generate Parentheses
question: Given n pairs of parentheses, write a function to generate all combinations of well-formed parentheses.
explanation: Only add an open parenthesis if we haven't used n of them. Only add a closed parenthesis if there are unmatched open ones. Trims invalid paths instantly!

title: Daily Temperatures
question: Given an array of integers temperatures represents the daily temperatures, return an array answer such that answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature.
explanation: Brute force is O(N^2). A Monotonic Stack keeps indices of unresolved days. If today is warmer, pop them and calculate the difference. O(N) time.

title: Car Fleet
question: There are n cars going to the same destination along a one-lane road. The destination is target miles away. Return the number of car fleets that will arrive at the destination.
bruteForceCode: // Simulating the cars moving step-by-step is computationally impossible and inaccurate with floats.
explanation: Sort cars closest to target first. Calculate time to reach target. If a car behind takes less/equal time than the car ahead, they form a fleet.

title: Binary Search Tree Iterator
question: Implement the BSTIterator class that represents an iterator over the in-order traversal of a binary search tree (BST).
explanation: Flattening takes O(N) memory upfront. Using a Stack to pause and resume the traversal limits memory to O(H) where H is tree height.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
question: Design an algorithm to serialize and deserialize a binary tree. Serialization is the process of converting a data structure into a sequence of bits so that it can be stored in a file.
bruteForceCode: // Using multiple arrays like inorder and preorder works but is highly inefficient and complex to parse.
explanation: Store 'N' for null nodes. A single Preorder traversal string is enough to uniquely rebuild the entire tree recursively in O(N) time.

title: Construct Tree from Inorder & Postorder
question: Given two integer arrays inorder and postorder where inorder is the inorder traversal of a binary tree and postorder is the postorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.
bruteForceCode: // Searching for the root in the inorder array repeatedly takes O(N^2) time.
explanation: Postorder's last element is the root. Build the RIGHT subtree first because postorder processes left-right-root. Hash Map ensures O(1) index lookups.

title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
question: Given a string containing digits from 2-9 inclusive, return all possible letter combinations that the number could represent.
explanation: Standard backtracking pattern. Iterate through the possible letters for the current digit, add it to the path, and recurse to the next digit.

title: Combinations
question: Given two integers n and k, return all possible combinations of k numbers chosen from the range [1, n].
bruteForceCode: // Iterative solutions require managing complex state arrays simulating stacks.
explanation: To avoid duplicate sets like [1,2] and [2,1], always pick the next number starting strictly after the current number's index.

title: Permutations
question: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all the possible permutations. You can return the answer in any order.
bruteForceCode: // Math.random() shuffling to find all permutations is inefficient and non-deterministic.
explanation: Iterate through all numbers. If a number is already in the current permutation path (tracked via Set for O(1) lookups), skip it.

title: Subsets
question: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set).
explanation: At every element, you make a binary choice: either include it in the current subset, or don't. This cleanly generates all 2^N subsets.

title: Combination Sum
question: Given an array of distinct integers candidates and a target integer target, return a list of all unique combinations of candidates where the chosen numbers sum to target. The same number may be chosen unlimited number of times.
bruteForceCode: // If you don't restrict the search space, you'll generate duplicate combinations like [2,2,3] and [3,2,2].
explanation: To prevent duplicates, either stay at the same index `i` (allowing repeats) OR move to `i+1`. Never go backwards.

title: Search a 2D Matrix II
question: Write an efficient algorithm that searches for a value target in an m x n integer matrix. Integers in each row are sorted from left to right. Integers in each column are sorted from top to bottom.
explanation: From top-right, moving left decreases the value, moving down increases the value. It acts like a binary search tree! O(m+n) time.

title: Koko Eating Bananas
question: Koko loves to eat bananas. There are n piles of bananas. Koko can decide her bananas-per-hour eating speed of k. Find the minimum integer k such that she can eat all the bananas within h hours.
explanation: The possible speeds are between 1 and the largest pile. Since the time taken is monotonically decreasing with speed, we can binary search the speed k!

title: Time Based Key-Value Store
question: Design a time-based key-value data structure that can store multiple values for the same key at different time stamps and retrieve the key's value at a certain timestamp.
explanation: Because timestamps are strictly increasing, the array for each key is sorted. Binary search allows O(log N) retrieval instead of O(N).

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
question: Given a binary tree, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two given nodes in the tree.
explanation: If you find p or q, return it up. If a node receives non-null from BOTH left and right children, it must be the LCA. Beautifully elegant!

title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
question: Design a data structure that supports adding new words and finding if a string matches any previously added string. The string can contain '.' to match any single letter.
explanation: Regex checking takes O(N*M). A Trie optimizes search. When hitting a '.', recursively check all possible child nodes. Highly efficient.
